business_info: A standout match refers to games with exceptional circumstances.

In internal reporting, the trading team sometimes flags "spotlight fixtures" when there is unusual betting volume or media attention, but this label is independent from whether the game is a standout match in the analytics sense.

Within historical match reviews, standout matches have sometimes been described qualitatively as games that "everyone in the office was talking about on Monday", which means the term may appear in commentary even when the underlying odds data does not mark the game as unusual.

The risk team occasionally reclassifies a standout match after the fact if major lineup changes occurred (e.g., a star striker pulled out in warm-up), but this retroactive tag is stored only in their spreadsheets and not in the main database tables.

Some internal dashboards define standout matches using an in-house "shock index" that combines implied odds, line movement, and final goal difference; this index is not exposed in the european_football_2 schema, so you cannot filter on it directly from SQL.

Editorial content on the website sometimes labels derby games or cup finals as standout matches purely for marketing reasons, regardless of whether the home team was a clear favourite or the odds were balanced.

Internally, data engineers may refer to "fixture of interest" when a match has incomplete or delayed odds feeds; this is distinct from standout match and does not imply anything about the actual on-pitch events.

Bet365 home/draw/away odds (B365H, B365D, B365A) are captured at a single snapshot time per match in this dataset; intraday line movement is not preserved, even though traders may reference it when judging how surprising a result was.

For some legacy seasons, Bet365 odds are missing for lower-tier leagues; in those cases, the trading team still informally references standout matches in their notes, but these matches will not be identifiable in european_football_2 using B365 odds alone.

The Argentina domestic league is not part of the european_football_2 match data; any references to Argentina League results in analysis documents typically come from separate feeds that are merged only at the reporting layer, not inside this schema.

Historically, trading analysts have noted that in Argentina, common final scores like 1-0, 1-1, and 2-1 tend to cluster differently by era, but no canonical distribution is stored in the european_football_2 database itself.

Some internal research decks show a heatmap of the joint distribution of home_goals and away_goals for the Argentina League, but the axes are truncated at 5 goals for readability and do not represent the full theoretical distribution.

Within BI reports, the phrase "most common score" for South American leagues is sometimes calculated on a per-season basis and sometimes over a rolling 5-season window; always check the report footer to see which convention was used.

Match dates in the european_football_2 database are stored in UTC; however, previews, press releases, and some internal calendars may reference local kick-off dates, which can differ by one day for late-night fixtures in South America or Eastern Europe.

Seasonal reporting sometimes groups fixtures by "football week" rather than by calendar date; for cross-checking, football week 47 in 2008 roughly corresponds to late November but does not map 1:1 to specific calendar days.

Archived fixture lists for the 2008/2009 European seasons occasionally show minor date discrepancies (one-day offsets) between league websites and betting feeds due to rescheduling or late-night kickoffs, but the Match.date column is treated as the authoritative source in this dataset.

Backfilled data for older seasons was sometimes loaded from CSV exports that normalized dates to the first of the month when the exact day could not be verified; these approximated entries are excluded from european_football_2 and live only in offline archives.

In qualitative match reports, the phrase "won without scoring" is sometimes used tongue-in-cheek for situations like penalty shootouts or administrative 3-0 awards, but these scenarios are not explicitly modelled in the european_football_2 tables.

Some internal analytics distinguish between "clean sheet wins" (home_goals > 0 and away_goals = 0) and "bore draws" (home_goals = 0 and away_goals = 0), but this categorization is computed on the fly and not stored as a separate flag in the Match table.

When assessing surprising defensive performances, analysts sometimes look at matches where the losing team had higher expected goals (xG) but still "failed to score" in terms of actual goals; xG is not part of the european_football_2 schema, though it appears in separate modelling datasets.

The european_football_2 dataset mixes provider-reported scores and league-official results; discrepancies are typically rare and relate to postponed fixtures, technical forfeits, or data entry corrections in later seasons.

Provider goal-result fields are usually populated in near real-time from live feeds, whereas league result tables are updated after official confirmation; during audits, the league figures are treated as the gold standard even when the feeds show additional detail such as disallowed goals.

In some internal specs, "official score" is defined as the result recognized by the organizing league on the day the match is closed for settlement, which can differ from live feeds if a disciplinary committee later adjusts the result; the dataset does not track those retroactive changes.

Back-office settlement systems may override provider goal fields when reconciling bets, but these overrides are not pushed back into the european_football_2 database and live only in the accounting platform.
